findings.description: parse() does list(reader) to fully materialize all rows, then builds a second full list of Record objects, doubling peak memory on large uploads instead of streaming row-by-row.
count_rows() reads the entire file via readlines() just to return len-1, using O(filesize) memory to compute a single integer that sum(1 for _ in f) would yield in O(1) memory.
stream_valid() advertises lazy yielding but calls parse(), which eagerly materializes 100% of records before the first yield, defeating the streaming contract.
count_rows() leaks the file handle (open() without with/close), relying on CPython refcount GC and risking descriptor exhaustion under repeated calls.
index dict is built from the header on every parse() call but never read (loop hardcodes row[0..3]), wasting an allocation per invocation.
